claims.text: On September 14, 2015 at 09:50:45 UTC, the two detectors of the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory simultaneously observed a transient gravitational-wave signal.
The transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 sweeps upwards in frequency from 35 to 250 Hz.
The transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 had a peak gravitational-wave strain of 1.0e-21.

The transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 matches the waveform predicted by general relativity for the inspiral and merger of a pair of black holes and the ringdown of the resulting single black hole.
The transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 was observed with a matched-filter signal-to-noise ratio of 24.

The transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 has a false alarm rate estimated to be less than 1 event per 203000 years, equivalent to a significance greater than 5.1 sigma.

The source of the transient gravitational-wave signal observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 lies at a luminosity distance of 410 Mpc, corresponding to a redshift of 0.09.

In the source frame, the two black holes whose merger produced the September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave signal detected by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory had initial masses of 36 and 29 solar masses.
In the source frame, the final black hole produced by the merger observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015 has a mass of 62 solar masses.
In the binary black hole merger observed by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory on September 14, 2015, 3.0 solar masses were radiated in gravitational waves.
All uncertainties reported for the September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave observation by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory define 90% credible intervals.
The observations of the September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave signal by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory demonstrate the existence of binary stellar-mass black hole systems.
The detection of the September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave signal by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory is the first direct detection of gravitational waves.
The September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave observation by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory is the first observation of a binary black hole merger.

theses.text: The detection of the September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave signal by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory is the first direct detection of gravitational waves.

The September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave signal detected by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory is the first observation of a binary black hole merger, matching the general-relativity waveform for the inspiral, merger, and ringdown of a pair of black holes.
The September 14, 2015 gravitational-wave observation by the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory demonstrates the existence of binary stellar-mass black hole systems.
